title: R v MILLER
text:
IN THE MATTER of the Drug Trafficking Act 1996
and
IN THE MATTER of JOHN MILLER
and
IN THE MATTER of the Petition dated 15th day of January 2003 
of William John Howarth Corlett Her Majestyâs Attorney General for the 
appointment of a Receiver



Petition




[1] By Petition dated 15th January 2003 brought under the Drug Trafficking Act 1996 (the Act), Her Majestyâs Attorney General seeks that this Court appoints a receiver over the assets of John Miller, and makes consequential variations to an order (Restraint Order) made on 15th September 1999 as varied, by which Mr. Millerâs assets are restrained. 




[2] Mr. Miller, and Shirley Kathleen Faragher were given notice of the Petition, which was called for first day on 11th February 2003. 




[3] The Petition was supported by an affidavit sworn on 15th January 2003 of Neil Stuart Mackie, and subsequently further supported by an affidavit sworn on 23rd January 2006 of Lindsey Jane Bermingham. 




Representation




[4] Mr. D. Griffin appeared for the Attorney General, and Miss E. Rattigan for Mr. Miller. At the hearing on 22nd May 2006, Mr. I. Kermode appeared for Ms. Faragher.












General Background




[5] On 15th September 2000, following trial by jury, Mr. Miller was sentenced to a term of custody for offences, inter alia, of drug trafficking. Mr. Miller was released from such custody on 28th February 2005. 




[6] During the course of the criminal proceedings, the Attorney General made application under section 2 of the Act. On 15th June 2001, following a contested hearing, at which, inter alia, Mr. Miller, and Ms. Faragher gave evidence, I found that Mr. Miller had benefited from drug trafficking, but adjourned determination of Mr. Millerâs realisable property, as defined in section 6 of the Act, pending further information relevant to Mr. Millerâs late fatherâs estate. On 27th July 2001, I certified that Mr. Miller had benefited from drug trafficking in the sum of £34,349.26, and made a confiscation order (Confiscation Order) in such amount.




[7] By orders made by consent on 14th October 2003, and 16th February 2005, the Restraint Order was varied to permit Mr. Miller to purchase a pick-up vehicle, and to expend an amount not exceeding £3,000 on the purchase of garden implements, and equipment to enable him to pursue self-employment as a gardener upon his release from custody. Such latter order also provided that the Confiscation Order was to be satisfied by 30th June 2005. On that day, I made a further order by consent that the Confiscation Order should be satisfied by 30th January 2007, and that Mr. Miller should pay £500.00 on 26th day of each month commencing on 26th July 2005.




[8] As at 22nd May 2006, Mr. Miller had paid £25,422,82, leaving the principal amount of £8,426.44 with interest thereon in the sum of £2,360.22. I record that the Attorney General does not seek interest from 14th October 2003. I further record that the majority of the monies paid towards satisfying the Confiscation Order have derived from Mr. Millerâs late fatherâs estate, and that since the Order of 30th June 2005, Mr. Miller has paid £1,500.00 by three trenches of £500.00 on 12th August 2005, 9th March 2006, and 2nd May 2006.




Evidence




[9] Whilst Mr. Miller had not filed an Answer he had filed his affidavit sworn on 20th March 2006. At the hearing on 7th April 2006, Mr. Miller confirmed the contents of his affidavit, in which he deposed that, since his release from custody, he had had gainful self employment, but that he had experienced more difficulty obtaining work than he had anticipated. He estimated that his total income for the period from his release from custody to the end of January 2006 was approximately £8,000. He stated that he had purchased a pickup vehicle, gardening equipment, and tools during April/May 2005. During the period May to August 2005, he had had sufficient work to enable him to save £2,500 towards the cost of advertising in the âYellow Pagesâ. However, from August 2005 to February 2006 he had had little, or no work. During February 2006, he had begun to receive work enquiries. Mr. Miller expanded upon statements in his affidavit as to his earnings, and his anticipated income with specific examples of work undertaken, and breakdown of gross, and net receipts therefrom. For the then current month of April, Mr. Miller stated that he had only one dayâs work certain. He was unable to give any evidence of meaningful projected income. However, he felt confident that he would be able to satisfy the Confiscation Order, but was unable to support such confidence by any evidence whatsoever. He stated that his assets consisted of the pickup, and the tools, and equipment used in his business.




[10] At the end of Mr. Millerâs evidence, being mindful of section 29(5), and (6) of the Act, I adjourned the hearing to afford Ms. Faragher the opportunity to take legal advice, and to make representations. I also granted Mr. Miller, and Ms. Faragher leave to file affidavits no later than 15th May 2006. 




[11] Prior to the adjourned hearing on 22nd May 2006, Mr. Miller had not filed a further affidavit. Ms. Faragher had filed her affidavit sworn on 15th May 2006, in which she deposed as to her former relationship with Mr. Miller, to whom she has two children, both now adults. I refer to paragraphs 7 to 11, and 16 of such affidavit, which paragraphs read:




â7. During my relationship with Mr Miller I have always worked. I have always paid the mortgage. I was the person who was responsible for earning, paying the mortgage and all outgoings and supporting out [sic] two children. Mr Miller contributed virtually nothing over the years. There was a brief period when Mr Miller operated a gardening business when he did have earnings and for that brief period he did contribute. Basically all expenses were paid by me. Mr Miller has spent many years in prison leaving me to provide for and raise our two children.




8. On 10 May 1999 I received a cheque in the sum of £13,400 from the Advocates who were dealing with the Estate of Mr Millerâs father Eric Kelly of which Mr Miller was a beneficiary. I believe that this cheque was an advance payment of what was to be due to Mr Miller. My understanding of the advancement was that Mr Miller considered this to be his contribution to the financial responsibilities he failed to meet throughout our relationship. I honestly considered it to be payment for what was owing to me and our children. I had supported both myself, Mr Miller and the children for many years.




9. Of the £13,400 on 17 May 1999 I gave Mr Miller £3,000 in cash from the monies received. Mr Miller needed money and we had agreed that I would give him £3,000 and the remainder belonged to me for the reasons I have detailed above.




10. The money has long been spent on maintaining my family and the household. There is none of it left.




11. I am presently in receipt of Job Seekers Allowance of £158.20 per fortnight. This is my only income.


â¦




16. The monies Mr Miller gave me in 1999 were monies that were more than due and owing to me in support of our children. Further, these monies have been long spent.â




Ms. Faragher then continued by disclosing her monthly expenditure, which exceeds her income.




[12] Other than her affidavit, Ms. Faragher did not give evidence. She was, however, represented by Mr. Kermode at such adjourned hearing. 




[13] I record that Ms. Faragherâs evidence in these proceedings was similar, but appears not to be identical, to oral evidence given during the hearing, which led to my judgment of 15th June 2001. At paragraphs 35 to 45 of that judgment, I referred in some detail to Ms. Faragherâs evidence. I refer to paragraphs 100 to104 of such judgment, which paragraphs read:




â100. As to the amount of £13,400 paid by Dickinson Cruickshank & Co from the estate of [Mr. Millerâs] late father to Ms Faragher at the direction of [Mr. Miller], [Mr. Millerâs] evidence and indeed that of Ms Faragher was that such sum was paid by [Mr. Miller] as a contribution towards monies expended by Ms Faragher in the upbringing of their children Barnaby and Jade. Both Ms Faragher and [Mr. Miller] stated that there had been no calculation as to how such sum was made up. Ms Faragher stated that she anticipated that [Mr. Miller] would have contributed to such expenditure when he âfelt he could do soâ and that the monies from his late fatherâs estate in the sum of £13400 was the first opportunity [Mr. Miller] had had to make such a contribution.




101. When he was arrested in 1999 [Mr. Miller] had upon his person the sum of £2340.76. When he was arrested in 1995 again for drug offences, [Mr. Miller] had in excess of £3000 in cash upon his person. [Mr. Miller], however, had made no contribution towards such referred to expenditure from any such monies. [Mr. Miller] would lead the Court to believe that the notebooks and diaries evidenced money lending transactions effected by him for which he might or might not have charged interest. Yet according to [Mr. Miller] and Ms Faragher he was not able until May 1999 to make any contribution to the upbringing of his children, Barnaby or Jade. I remind myself that during her evidence Ms Faragher stated that there was no order of court requiring [Mr. Miller] to pay any periodical or other payments to Ms Faragher relevant to Barnaby or Jade.




102 I refer to the previously cited copy correspondence telephone notes and file notes from the file of Dickinson & Cruickshank & Co. I accept that [Mr. Millerâs] initial enquiry relevant to the progress of the winding up of his late fatherâs estate took place prior to his arrest on 24th April 1999. However, by 5th May 1999 [Mr Miller] had been arrested for drug trafficking offences. I refer particularly to the above cited memorandum of the telephone conversations of 5th May 1999 between [Mr. Miller] and Dickinson & Cruickshank & Co. I pause to comment that [Mr. Miller] did not challenge directly or indirectly the accuracy of such memorandum. The same records that [Mr. Miller] sought payment of the partial distribution of the residuary estate to him in cash. When he was informed that a cash payment was not possible and was requested to name a person who could facilitate the payment of the distribution to him, he suggested Ms Faragher.




103 I remind myself that [Mr. Miller] had previously been convicted of drug trafficking offences and had been the subject of a confiscation order. As at 5th May 1999 [Mr. Miller] had been arrested for drug trafficking offences the subject matter drug being Methylenedioxymethylamphetamine a controlled drug of Class A.




104 I find that the sum of £13400 caused by [Mr. Miller] to be paid to Ms Faragher was not paid under any obligation by [Mr. Miller]. I reject without hesitation [Mr. Millerâs] and Ms Faragherâs evidence that such payment was by way of contribution to Ms Faragher for monies expended by her relevant to Barnaby and Jade or indeed any other expenditure. I find that such monies were a gift by [Mr. Miller] to Ms Faragher and are therefore realisable property.â




Conclusions




[14] Although neither Mr. Miller nor Ms. Faragher sought to argue delay on the part of the Attorney General seeking to enforce the Confiscation Order, Mr. Griffin raised the matter, and submitted that there had been no delay. Since the Petition was filed on 15th January 2003, there have been vacations of hearing dates, and adjournments at Mr. Millerâs request. Further, in the interim, there have been voluntary payments by Mr. Miller. Mr. Griffin further submitted that as soon as it became clear that Mr. Miller was not complying with the 30th June 2005 Order, the Attorney General sought a hearing of the Petition. In support of such submissions, Mr. Griffin referred to Leonard Lloyd v Bow Street Magistrates Court [2003] EWHC 2294 (Admin), and in particular that part of the judgment of Dyson LJ at paragraph 27, which part reads:




âIt follows that, in deciding what is a reasonable time, regard should be had to the efforts made to extract the money by other methods, for example (as in the present case) the appointment of a receiverâ¦â¦ Likewise, if the defendant has been evasive and has avoided diligent attempts to extract the money from him, he will be unable to rely on the resultant delay in support of an argument that his right to a determination within a reasonable time has been violatedâ




Mr Griffin argued that delay in appointing a receiver could lead to criticism of the Attorney General. He further referred to Attorney Generalâs Reference(No 2 of 2001) [2004]1 Cr App R 25; and Crowther v The United Kingdom 53741/00 [2005] ECHR 45. I accept such submissions, and find that any delayhas been at the request, and for the benefit of Mr. Miller.




[15] It was common ground that the statutory requirements under section 29(1) of the Act had been met, namely the Confiscation Order had been made, it has not been satisfied, and it is not subject to appeal. It was further common ground that the Court has a discretion whether to appoint a receiver in respect of Mr. Millerâs realisable property.




[16] Miss Rattigan submitted that a significant proportion of the Confiscation Order has been paid, that Mr. Miller has gainful self-employment, and is confident that he will be able to satisfy such order, that in such circumstances the appointment of a receiver would be unnecessarily burdensome, and add considerably to the expense of the matter generally. Miss Rattigan suggested that, if the Court is minded to appoint a receiver, then it would be preferable to afford Mr. Miller the opportunity to prove that he could satisfy the Confiscation Order by making an unless order by which a receiver would be appointed if Mr. Miller failed to meet any future instalment.




[17] Mr. Kermode submitted that if a receiver is appointed, he will be entitled to pursue all Mr. Millerâs realisable property, which might mean that Ms. Faragher looses her home: it would be unfair upon Ms. Faragher to appoint a receiver. 




[18] At the conclusion of the hearing on 22nd May 2006, I rejected Miss Rattiganâs and Mr. Kermodeâs submissions, and determined to appoint a receiver. 




[19] I refer to section 31 of the Act, and, in particular those provisions which read:




â31 Exercise by High Court or receiver of powers for the realisation of property




(1) The following provisions apply to the powers conferred â




(a) on the High Court by sections 26 to 30; â¦.




(2) Subject to subsections (3) to (6), the powers shall be exercised with a view to making available for satisfying the confiscation order or, as the case may be, any confiscation order that may be made in the defendant's case, the value for the time being of realisable property held by any person, by means of the realisation of such property.




(3) In the case of realisable property held by a person to whom the defendant has directly or indirectly made a gift caught by this Act, the powers shall be exercised with a view to realising no more than the value for the time being of the gift.




(4) The powers shall be exercised with a view to allowing any person other than the defendant or the recipient of any such gift to retain or recover the value of any property held by him.




(5) In exercising the powers, no account shall be taken of any obligations of the defendant or of the recipient of any such gift which conflict with the obligation to satisfy the confiscation order.




â¦â¦â¦â¦â




[20] I agree with Mr. Griffin that the Court when exercising its discretion should be guided by the legislative steer provided by section 31, which steer includes those given by subsections (2), and (5). Mr. Miller has been given every opportunity to satisfy the Confiscation Order. He has sought, and obtained extensions of time. He has made proposals for instalment payments, for example his proposals which led to the 30th June 2005 Order, subsequent to which instead of paying in the aggregate £5,000, he has paid £1,500 by 22nd May 2006. Mr. Miller has not applied to vary such Order. He has not applied for a certificate of inadequacy under section 17 of the Act. Whilst Mr. Miller professed confidence that he will satisfy the Confiscation Order, he was unable to provide any meaningful evidence of projected earnings, or to suggest any realistic proposals to reduce the arrears of instalments under the 30th June 2005 Order, and/or to maintain regular payments. 




[21] I refer to those parts of section 29 of the Act, which read:


â29 Realisation of property


â¦â¦..


(2) The court may appoint a receiver in respect of realisable property.


(3) The court may empower a receiver appointed under subsection (2) under section 26 or in pursuance of a charging order -




â¦â¦â¦â¦




(b) in relation to any realisable property â¦â¦â¦to take possession of the property subject to such conditions or exceptions as may be specified by the court.




(4) The court may order any person having possession of realisable property to give possession of it to any such receiver.




(5) The court may empower any such receiver to realise any realisable property in such manner as the court may direct.




(6) The court may -




(a) order any person holding an interest in realisable property to make to the receiver such payment as it may direct in respect of any beneficial interest held by the defendant or, as the case may be, the recipient of a gift caught by this Act; and




(b) on the payment being made, by order transfer, grant or extinguish any interest in the property.




.â¦â¦..




(8) The court shall not in respect of any property exercise the powers conferred by subsection (3)(a), (5) or (6) unless a reasonable opportunity has been given for persons holding any interest in the property to make representations to the court.â




[22] This Court afforded Ms. Faragher a reasonable opportunity to make representations, in response she filed her affidavit. In such affidavit, Ms. Faragher deposes that âof the £13,400 on 17th May 1999 I gave Mr. Miller £3,000 in cash from the monies received. Mr. Miller needed the money and we agreed that I would give him £3,000 and the remainder belonged to meâ. In my judgment of 15th June 2001, I found that Mr. Miller had gifted £13,400 to Ms. Faragher. I record that I made no reference to Ms Faragher having asserted that she had given back £3,000 to Mr. Miller.




[23] If the best way of enforcing a Confiscation Order is the appointment of a receiver, then an appointment lies. In this case, having regard to all matters, I had no hesitation concluding that the best way to enforce the Confiscation Order is to appoint a receiver. Thus at the conclusion of the hearing, I gave my decision that I would appoint Mr. Roger Barrs of Howarth Clark Whitehall, Chartered Accountants as Receiver in respect of Mr. Millerâs realisable property. As to the terms, powers, and conditions of Mr. Barrsâ appointment, I look to assistance from Counsel.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
party_one: HER MAJESTY'S ATTORNEY GENERAL OF THE ISLE OF MAN
